coredata.dc:title: Fabricating nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) on Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 nanospheres: A nanoheterostructure for enhanced photocatalytic performance for water purification
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Physics and Chemistry of Solids
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Wei
Yang, Shuang
Liu, Yanan
Shi, Chunli
Shi, Weilong
Lin, Xue
Guo, Feng
Hong, Yuanzhi
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we first fabricated Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 nanospheres by using a simple hydrothermal process and then anchored nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) on them through a facile deposition–precipitation process. The binary NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 nanocomposites exhibited enhanced photocatalytic activity for the degradation of rhodamine B (Rh B) and bisphenol A (BPA), in which NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 with a mass ratio of 0.25 % exhibits the best photocatalytic activity. The excellent photocatalytic property of this composite can be attributed to the synergistic effect between the NCDs and Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55. Moreover, anchoring NCDs on the nanospheres improved their efficiency of harvesting light, and the NCDs served as reaction sites during photocatalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Heterostructure
Photocatalysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 31971616 , 22006057 ), the “Doctor of Mass Entrepreneurship and Innovation” Project of Jiangsu Province, the Doctoral Scientific Research Foundation of Jiangsu University of Science and Technology (China) ( 1062931806 ), the Youth Talent Lifting Project of Jilin Province ( 181907 ), the Science and Technology Innovation Development Plan of Jilin City ( 20190104120 ), the Science and Technology Research Project of the Department of Education of Jilin Province ( JJKH20200039KJ ), and the Science and Technology Development Project of Jilin Province ( YDZJ202101ZYTS070 , 20200301046RQ ).

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31971616, 22006057), the ?Doctor of Mass Entrepreneurship and Innovation? Project of Jiangsu Province, the Doctoral Scientific Research Foundation of Jiangsu University of Science and Technology (China) (1062931806), the Youth Talent Lifting Project of Jilin Province (181907), the Science and Technology Innovation Development Plan of Jilin City (20190104120), the Science and Technology Research Project of the Department of Education of Jilin Province (JJKH20200039KJ), and the Science and Technology Development Project of Jilin Province (YDZJ202101ZYTS070, 20200301046RQ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 (a) and 0.25 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 (b).
SEM images of 0.25 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 (a) and the corresponding EDS elemental mapping images of 0.25 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 sample (b–f).
TEM image of NCDs (a), TEM (b) and HRTEM (c) images of the as-prepared 0.25 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 sample.
The high-resolution XPS spectra of the as-prepared 0.25 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 sample.
(a) UV–vis spectra of Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 and 0.25 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55.(b) Plots of (ahv)2 versus photon energy (hv) for the band gap energies of the as-obtained Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55.

(a) The PL spectra of Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 and 0.25 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 at excitation wavelength of 325 nm (b) Photocurrent response versus time of as-prepared Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 and 0.25 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 in 0.1 M Na2SO4 under xenon lamp irradiation (0.3 V vs. SCE, 100 mW cm−2).(c) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm curves of the as-prepared samples.

(a) Photodegradation efficiencies of Rh B as a function of irradiation time for different photocatalysts. (b) Photodegradation efficiencies of BPA as a function of irradiation time for different photocatalysts. (c) Cycling runs for the photocatalytic degradation of Rh B over the 0.25 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 photocatalyst under visible light illumination.

(a) Trapping experiment of active species during the photocatalytic degradation of Rh B by 0.25 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 photocatalyst under visible light irradiation. DMPO spin-trapping ESR spectra for 0.25 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 for DMPO-•OH (b) and DMPO-•O2− (c).
Possible mechanism of the enhanced photocatalytic activity in the NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 nanocomposite.
Illustration of the synthesis of the NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 composite.

head.ce:title: Fabricating nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) on Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 nanospheres: A nanoheterostructure for enhanced photocatalytic performance for water purification

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Energy and Power, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang, 212003, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we first fabricated Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 nanospheres by using a simple hydrothermal process and then anchored nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) on them through a facile deposition–precipitation process. The binary NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 nanocomposites exhibited enhanced photocatalytic activity for the degradation of rhodamine B (Rh B) and bisphenol A (BPA), in which NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 with a mass ratio of 0.25 % exhibits the best photocatalytic activity. The excellent photocatalytic property of this composite can be attributed to the synergistic effect between the NCDs and Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55. Moreover, anchoring NCDs on the nanospheres improved their efficiency of harvesting light, and the NCDs served as reaction sites during photocatalysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental problems and the energy crisis have emerged as issues of global concern in recent years. Scientists from countries all over the world have made efforts to solve these problems [1–4]. This requires controlling environmental pollution while ensuring sustainable economic development [5,6]. Photocatalysis has been widely studied by researchers in recent years due to its ability to decompose organic pollutants, but improving photocatalytic efficiency remains a challenging problem [7–11]. Bi-based oxides exhibit excellent photocatalytic performance owing to their upward valence band (VB), and small band gap [12–14]. Bismuth molybdate compounds, including Bi2MoO6 [12], Bi2Mo3O12 [15,16], Bi2Mo2O9 [17], and Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 [18], have attracted considerable attention in research to this end. We have also investigated Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 in past work in the area [19,20]. However, as in case of other semiconductors, the poor visible-light harvesting and high recombination rate of photoinduced charges are hurdles to increase the photocatalytic efficiency of pure Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55. Although traditional, single-component photocatalysis is widely used, it suffers from problems of unsteadiness and the swift compounding of electron–hole pairs [21,22]. To overcome these shortcomings, exploring efficient visible-light components and establishing a complete system of the photocatalysis of binary composites have emerged as popular subjects of research on photocatalysis [21].

Compared with the tradition, one-component photocatalyst, a two-phase heterostructure has the following advantages: (1) It has higher efficiency of visible-light absorption. (2) The band gap of the heterostructure can be fixed by changing the catalyst added. (3) The charge transfer and separation between semiconductors are satisfactory. A number of Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55-based composite photocatalysts, such as Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55/Bi2MoO6, RGO-Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55, Ag3PO4/Ag/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55, and g-C3N4/Ag/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 heterojunctions, are known to significantly enhance photocatalytic performance [20–24].

N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs), a recently developed, zero-dimensional nanocarbon material that has attracted significant interest owing to its unique electronic and optical properties, have been used as a catalyst or a co-catalyst [25]. When NCDs are combined with another semiconductor, the visible-light absorption of the resulting composite increases [26], and it can produce more photogenerated charges to deliver better photocatalytic performance [27–29]. Sim et al. reported an increase in the efficiency of photoelectrochemically produced H2 after the modification of the Si photocathode with N-doped CQDs (NCDs). Under the same test conditions, the NCDs delivered better performance than monolayer graphene and other carbon-based materials such as g-C3N4 [30]. Electrons inside the conduction band (CB) of a semiconductor can move more easily to N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs), which is propitious for the transfer of photogenerated charges. A combination of NCDs and a semiconductor is expected to significantly improve photocatalytic performance [31,32]. NCDs loaded onto the heterostructure can improve its efficiency of harvesting light, and act as reaction site in the photocatalytic process [33,34].

Through the synergistic effect of NCDs and Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55, the NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 heterostructure can enhance the visible light-driven photocatalytic degradation of organic contaminants. It can simultaneously increase the specific surface area and enhance the capability of the structure to capture visible light, which improves the separation of the photogenerated electron–hole pairs to a greater extent than pure Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55. Moreover, no study to date has examine the photocatalytic performance of the NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 composite, to the best of our knowledge. In this study, NCD-loaded Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 nanospheres were fabricated through a deposition–precipitation method. The photocatalytic activity of the composite was evaluated in terms of the photodegradation of rhodamine B (Rh B) and bisphenol A (BPA) under visible light. The rates of photocatalytic degradation of Rh B and BPA by the two-component NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 nanocomposite were found to be higher than those obtained by using clean Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 nanospheres. The possible photocatalytic mechanism of the NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 nanospheres is discussed and analyzed according to the results of characterization. Moreover, we study the reaction-free radical and the stability of the photocatalyst.

The NCDs were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method. Briefly, citric acid (1.752 g) was added to 17 mL of deionized water and mixed thoroughly (30 min) to yield a homogeneous suspension. Then, 560 μL of ethylenediamine was added to the suspension and magnetically stirred for another 30 min. The combined suspension was then transferred to a 50-mL Teflon-lined steel autoclave and heated at 250 °C for 5 h. The as-obtained aqueous suspension was then centrifuged at 10000 rpm and dialyzed in a dialysis bag to eliminate large nanoparticles. The as-obtained NCDs were kept at 10 °C for characterization and further use.

Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 nanospheres were fabricated via the simple hydrothermal technology reported in a previous study [22]. The NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 nanocomposites were synthesized through a facile deposition–precipitation method. To load the NCDs onto the Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 nanospheres, 100 mg of as-obtained Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 nanospheres were immersed in 50 mL of DI water. Then, a certain amount the NCD solution (1 mg mL−1) was added dropwise to the above as-obtained solution while it was stirred. The reaction temperature was maintained at 40 °C for 60 min. The suspension was filtered, washed several times with deionized water, and dried in vacuum at 70 °C. The theoretical magnitudes of loading of the NCDs were 0.17, 0.25 and 0.50 wt%, respectively (denoted respectively by 0.17 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55, 0.25 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55, and 0.50 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55). The characterizations were obtained, and are provided in Supporting Information.

The strategy for the synthesis of the NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 nanospheres is clearly illustrated in Scheme 1 . The morphologies of the as-prepared Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 and the NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 composites were studied by SEM (Fig. 1 ). Fig. 1a and b shows that the pure Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 and the 0.25 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 had spherical morphologies with an average size of dozens of nanometers. The formation of the NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 nanoheterostructure was verified through the elemental mapping of the 0.25 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 (as shown in Fig. 2 b–f). Maps of Bi–M, V-L, O–K, N–K, and C–K had an identical shape and site, verifying the presence of NCDs and Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 in the as-prepared NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 composite. This provided reasonable proof for the emergence of the NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 nanoheterostructure. Figs. S1a and b show SEM images of the as-fabricated 0.17 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 and the 0.50 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 composites, respectively. Once they had been loaded with the NCDs, the morphologies of the 0.17 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 and 0.50 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 were found to have undergone almost no significant change owing to the small size and number of NCDs.

Fig. 3 a shows a TEM image of the NCDs, which shows that the as-synthesized NCDs were monodispersed in the aqueous solution, and each quantum dot had the same size. The TEM image shows a spherical nanostructure of the as-fabricated 0.25 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 sample (Fig. 3b). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the 0.25 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 (Fig. 2c) shows NCDs on the surface of Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55. A lattice fringe spacing of 0.32 nm was assigned to the anatase (111) facet of Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 [22], whereas a lattice fringe spacing of 0.21 nm was attributed to the (100) facet of the NCDs [39]. Fig. S2 shows the PL spectra and UV–vis absorption of the NCDs. The as-prepared NCDs sample had an absorption band at 425 nm, approximately (Fig. S1a). As is shown in Fig. S2b, under UV light, the NCDs emitted intense blue fluorescence at roughly 440 nm through one-photon excitation. Further, they dispersed on the colorimetric dish while emitting blue light through one-photon excitation.

The phases and crystal structures of the NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 composite and clear Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 were investigated by XRD. Fig. S3 shows that pure Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 could be readily indexed to Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 (JCPDS no. 14–0688). Loading the NCDs on it did not significantly change the crystal structure of Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55. While it has been reported that the NCDs display an inferior but characteristic diffraction peak around 26°, this peak was not prominent in the patterns of the NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 composite, possibly because of their small number and relatively weak intensity of diffraction in the composite [39].

To survey the chemical composition and chemical states of the obtained samples, high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s, Bi 4f, and Mo 3d of the 0.25 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 sample are shown in Fig. 4 a–d. The C1s spectrum was fitted with three peaks centered at 282.7, 283.3, and 286.6 eV (Fig. 4a), corresponding to the C–C, C–O, and CN3 bonds, respectively, in the heterocycle ring of the NCDs [40–42]. The N 1s spectrum could be deconvoluted into two bonds involving triazine rings (C–NC, 396.4 eV) and tertiary nitrogen (NC3), 398.9 eV) (Fig. 4b). The N atoms were successfully doped into the framework of the NCDs. As is shown in Fig. 4c and d, the binding energies of Bi 4f 7/2 and Bi 4f 5/2 were 157.7 and 163.0 eV, respectively, and matched with those of Bi3+ [24]. Those of Mo 3d were 230.7 eV and 233.9 eV [43,44]. The XPS spectrum of the pure Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 sample is shown in Fig. S4. Compared with the pure Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 sample, a slight shift in the binding energy of Bi 4f and Mo 3d was observed for the 0.25 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55, implying interaction between Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 and the NCDs.

The UV–vis DRS spectra are shown in Fig. 5 a, and were used to study the ocular absorption of the NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 composite. The onset of absorption of Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 was at around 460 nm; thus, the band gap of Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 was determined to be 2.70 eV (Fig. 5b). In comparison with pure Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55, the 0.25 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 had an absorption in the visible range that occurred with a red shift along the edge of absorption owing to the synergistic effect of Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 and NCDs. The as-prepared NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 composite could thus be used as a visible-light photocatalyst.

The Fermi energy level was calculated by using UPS, and the spectra of Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 were recorded as shown in Figs. S5a and b. The ionization potential of Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55, which was equivalent to E VB, was −6.9 eV versus that in a vacuum. The value of E CB of Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 was −4.2 eV vs. that in a vacuum, according to E CB = E VB - E g. The value of E CB of Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 was −0.24 V vs. the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE), and the value of E VB of Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 was 2.46 V vs. RHE. This follows the standard whereby 0 V vs. RHE is equal to −4.44 eV vs. that in a vacuum (Fig. S5c). As is shown in Fig. 6 a, the 0.25 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 composite exhibited a much weaker PL intensity than clean Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55, showing a lower rate of recombination of the binary composite [35–40]. This might have resulted from the formation of nanoheterojunctions at the interface of Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 and the NCDs. The chronoamperometric I–t curves are shown in Fig. 6b. In relation to pure Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55, the 0.25 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 exhibited strengthened photocurrent responses, suggesting the faster charge carries separation efficiency of 0.25 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 [45,46]. The nanoheterojunctions on the interface of Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 and the NCDs facilitate photoelectron–hole separation and thus retarded the photoelectron–hole recombination of the binary composite. In addition, Fig. S6 shows the EIS of Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 and 0.25 % CNDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55., where the latter had a smaller arc radius than the former. It reveals the low resistance to charge transfer and enhanced interfacial electron transport of the 0.25 % CNDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.5. The BET surface areas of pure Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 and 0.25 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 were analyzed using an automated surface area analyzer (Fig. 6c). The calculated BET surface area of 0.25 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 was 109.52 m2 g−1, larger than that of the single Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 (76.15 m2 g−1). We can conclude that the loading of NCDs facilitated a more efficient contact between the NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 composite and organic contaminants, which improved photocatalytic performance [41].

By using the Rh B dye and BPA as target contaminants, we evaluated the photocatalytic performance of the prepared samples. Fig. 7 a shows that the experimental process reached equilibrium after 40 min. Pure Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 had relatively poor photocatalytic activity (53 %). However, after the modification of Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 with the NCDs, the photocatalytic performance of the NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 composites was significantly enhanced, and the 0.25 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 sample showed the highest efficiency (80 %) among all binary composites. Too large or too small a load of the co-catalyst reduced catalytic activity. This is because the loading of the NCDs on Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 can result in the efficient separation of photoinduced charges, which helps increase photocatalytic activity. On the contrary, loading too many NCDs may block the transfer of the photogenerated electrons and reduce the exposure of active sites owing to the high overlap between NCDs. This is consistent with the results of past work [47]. Furthermore, BPA was used to examine the photodegradation (Fig. 7b). The experimental results showed similar trends of degradation of Rh B under the same conditions. However, the pH of the solution significantly affected the photocatalytic degradation of antibiotics. Fig. S7 and Table 1 show the effect of pH on the removal of Rh B for 0.25 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55. Under acidic conditions (pH = 2.0), the 0.25 % CNDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 was more photoactive toward the Rh B solution. Fig. 7c shows measurements of the photocatalytic stability of 0.25 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55. After three cycles, its photocatalytic efficiency for Rh B slightly decreased, confirming that the 0.25 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 photocatalyst was stable under visible-light irradiation. As shown in Fig. S8, barely 1.79 % of the TOC was reduced in the Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 photocatalyst while 6.15 % of it was removed by using 0.25 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55. This suggests that the latter composites more efficiently degraded Rh B.

We used three sacrificial agents—ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na), isopropanol (IPA), and benzoquinone (BQ)—as •OH, h+, and •O2 − scavengers, respectively (Fig. 8 a). If IPA and BQ were added to the reaction system, the rates of degradation of Rh B for the 0.25 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 photocatalyst decreased significantly. These results show that •OH and •O2 − play an important role in photocatalysis. Furthermore, DMPO spin-trapping ESR spectra were used to examine the roles of •OH and •O2 − with the 0.25 % NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 composite throughout the photodegradation procedure (as shown in Fig. 8b and c). When the light was turned on, the characteristic signals of DMPO-•OH and DMPO-•O2 − were clearly detected. Therefore, the results of the free radical trapping experiment and ESR show that •O2 − and •OH were the main active substances in the reaction.

This study fabricated binary NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 nanoheterostructures with a distinct content of NCDs. The NCDs/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 nanoheterostructure showed enhanced photocatalytic activity under visible-light illumination because the loaded NCDs improved the BET surface, visible-light harvesting, and the separation between photogenerated electrons and holes. This resulted in higher photocatalytic activity than before. This novel catalyst provides theoretical support for improving the photocatalytic activity of binary composites.

Wei Sun: Validation, Data Curation. Shuang Yang: Writing - Original Draft. Yanan Liu: Investigation.Chunli Shi: Writing - Review & Editing. Weilong Shi: Resources, Formal analysis. Xue Lin: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Feng Guo: Funding acquisition. Yuanzhi Hong: Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31971616, 22006057), the “Doctor of Mass Entrepreneurship and Innovation” Project of Jiangsu Province, the Doctoral Scientific Research Foundation of Jiangsu University of Science and Technology (China) (1062931806), the Youth Talent Lifting Project of Jilin Province (181907), the Science and Technology Innovation Development Plan of Jilin City (20190104120), the Science and Technology Research Project of the Department of Education of Jilin Province (JJKH20200039KJ), and the Science and Technology Development Project of Jilin Province (YDZJ202101ZYTS070, 20200301046RQ).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpcs.2021.110283.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4 quantum dots/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 nanospheres composites

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yang, S., Liu, C., Wang, J., Lin, X., Hong, Y., Guo, F., & Shi, J., Enhanced photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4 quantum dots/Bi3.64Mo0.36O6.55 nanospheres composites, Journal of Solid State Chemistry 287 (2020) 121347.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication of a ternary carbon dots/CoO/g‐C3N4 nanocomposite photocatalyst with enhanced visible‐light‐driven photocatalytic hydrogen production

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rapid polymerization synthesizing high-crystalline g-C3N4 towards boosting solar photocatalytic H2 generation
